steps.title: Support two formation phases
steps.content: This is a Command of Evidence question. The researchers claim the thick disk formed in two distinct phases rather than one. We need a finding that directly supports the idea of two separate formation events — meaning evidence of two different time periods within the thick disk itself.

steps.title: Rule out off-target choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'The telescopes used by the researchers have detected stars of similar ages in galaxies other than the Milky Way.': Compares to stars in other galaxies, not the thick disk of the Milky Way. Irrelevant to the claim about the thick disk's formation phases.
  • Choice C — 'The thin disk contains about twice as many stars that can be seen from Earth as the thick disk does.': Compares the thin and thick disks by star count. This is about quantity between disk types, not about formation timing within the thick disk.

steps.title: Rule out the remaining choices
steps.content: • Choice D — 'The stars in the Milky Way tend to have very similar chemical compositions.': Too broad — covers all Milky Way stars, not the thick disk specifically, and says nothing about formation timing.

steps.title: Choose the age-gap finding
steps.content:
• Choice B — 'There's an age difference of about 2 billion years between certain stars in the thick disk.': An age gap between stars within the thick disk means they did not all form at the same time — exactly what "two distinct phases" requires.
The answer is B.
